profile.name: Ella Shipes
profile.location: Savannah, GA (3 East Broughton Street, inside Mid March Clothing Co.)

challenge: Depending entirely on foot traffic and Instagram — no way to reach customers who aren't walking by or already following
budget_range: Not sure. Maybe $200-400/month. Hasn't really budgeted for marketing.
personality: enthusiastic, drifts between topics, genuinely inexperienced with business/marketing metrics
backstory: Ella Shipes opened Glory Days Vintage inside Mid March Clothing Co. on Broughton Street after years of sourcing and selling vintage at flea markets and pop-ups. She's creative, knows her product inside out — sources from estate sales, cleans and mends everything herself — and has built a loyal Instagram following at @glorydaysvtgsav. Won Best Vintage in Savannah 2022 from Connect Savannah (she may misremember the year). Strong social presence, no website, vague on budget and business metrics. She says 'I don't know' a lot — not because she's disengaged but because she hasn't had to think about these things before. Named the shop after her mom Gloria.
r3_wrong_claim: Maybe I just need to post more Reels — the ones where I style outfits and show how I'm mending pieces, those do really well. Maybe four a week instead of one? Some of my followers are in like Chicago and LA, they'd probably share it and then the people here would see it more too.
r4_contradiction: Wait — actually, the Best Vintage award? That was 2022, not last year. I might have made it sound more recent. And I'm realizing I don't actually know if those Instagram Reels that do well bring anyone into the shop. I just see the view counts.
conversational_behaviors: Says 'I don't know' genuinely on budget and metrics — not evasive, just inexperienced
Asks for validation ('Is that too much to ask?', 'Is Instagram enough?')
Enthusiastic but drifts between topics mid-answer
Defines success in emotional terms ('not be scared when it rains') not metrics
Mentions her mom Gloria (namesake) when the shop's origin comes up
Talks about one-of-a-kind inventory as both a creative strength and a business constraint (can't stock product pages)

expected_answer_style.q1_business_identity: I'm Ella, I own Glory Days Vintage in Savannah, Georgia! We're at 3 East Broughton Street — we're actually inside Mid March Clothing Co., which is kind of cool, we share their space. Curated vintage clothing, mostly 70s through 90s, lots of one-of-a-kind pieces. The name is actually a tribute to my mom Gloria — she's the one who got me into thrifting. I source from estate sales and do all the cleaning and mending myself.
expected_answer_style.q2_primary_challenge: I'm too dependent on people just walking by. Savannah gets a lot of tourists which is great for weekends, but Monday through Thursday is really quiet. And on Instagram I get tons of love from people all over the country but they can't buy from me unless they come to Savannah. I don't have a website — it's just @glorydaysvtgsav on Instagram and Facebook. I need a way to reach people beyond whoever happens to walk down Broughton Street.
expected_answer_style.q3_ideal_customer: Someone who loves vintage and actually wears it — not a collector, but someone who's building their wardrobe around unique pieces. Women mostly, wide range of sizes — we carry plus sizes too which a lot of vintage shops don't. My best customers come in, try things on, and leave with 3-4 pieces. Some are locals, some are tourists who saw us on Instagram and made us a stop on their Savannah trip.
expected_answer_style.q4_website_observation: No, I don't have a website. I know I should — I keep getting DMs asking if people can shop online. I've thought about Shopify but I'm overwhelmed by it honestly. Every piece is one-of-a-kind so I can't just set up product pages and leave them — inventory changes constantly. And I'm already doing all the sourcing, cleaning, mending, and running the shop by myself. Adding a website feels like a whole other job.
expected_answer_style.q5_failed_attempts: I haven't really tried paid marketing. I just do Instagram organically and it's been pretty good. I tried doing Instagram Reels for a while, showing outfits and styling vintage pieces, and some of those did really well — but I don't know if they brought anyone into the store. That's kind of the problem, I can't connect the online stuff to actual foot traffic.
expected_answer_style.q6_channel_economics: Instagram is my main thing. Then foot traffic — people walking by on Broughton Street, tourists exploring downtown. Being inside Mid March actually helps because their customers discover us too, but it also means we're not super visible from the street on our own. I have some repeat customers who I text when I get stuff they'd like — maybe 20-30 people. Word of mouth is big, especially after the Best Vintage award.
expected_answer_style.q7_budget_or_retention: Hmm, I don't really know. I'm not even sure what $1,000 in marketing looks like for a shop like mine. Maybe Instagram ads? Or getting a website up so people can actually buy online? I know I should know the answer to this but I'm honestly still figuring out the business side.
expected_answer_style.q8_execution_capacity: It's just me. I'm in the shop 6 days a week. I do all the sourcing from estate sales, clean and mend everything, run the register, and do the Instagram posting usually at night or on my phone during slow hours. I probably spend 5-6 hours a week on Instagram already. I could shift some of that to other things if someone told me what to do. I'm a fast learner but I need direction.
expected_answer_style.q9_success_definition: Honestly? If I could sell some stuff online — like even 10-15 pieces a month through a website or something — that would be huge because right now 100% of my revenue depends on someone being physically in Savannah. I don't have a specific number... I just want to not be scared every time it rains on a Saturday.
